key_insight: Adding site-projected orbital density of states (pDOS) to atomic graph-node features gives graph neural networks direct electronic-structure context, substantially improving materials-property prediction.
engineering_relevance: This offers a practical route to higher-accuracy screening of functional materials by combining DFT-derived electronic fingerprints with crystal-graph learning.
method: Compute pDOS fingerprints from density functional theory for each atomic site.
Fuse pDOS with Pettifor elemental embeddings before message passing in a graph neural network.
Use an interpretable spectral attention-gating module to identify the most relevant orbitals and energy windows.
evidence_signal: Compared with an elemental-descriptor baseline, pDOS augmentation reduced prediction error by 22.9% for superconducting Tc and 27.9% for optical dielectric constant ε∞, with gains comparable to doubling the training set.
watch_item: Benefits depend on dataset size; for smaller training sets the pDOS vector can be too high-dimensional relative to available data, limiting improvement.
paper_title: Machine Learning Materials Properties by Encoding Orbital-Projected Density of States
